metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — confirmed by return address on envelope (R.E. Hanifen, 3614 Noble St., Dallas, Texas) and signed 'Your Brother Bob'
metadata.to: John Hanifen (PFC John R. Hanifen, serving in Korea)
metadata.location_sent_to: PFC John R. Hanifen 55046284, Co. C, 439 Eng. Com. Bn., APO 301, c/o PM, San Francisco, Calif.
metadata.envelope_notes: Air mail envelope with two 3-cent Thomas Jefferson stamps. Return address: R.E. Hanifen, 3614 Noble St., Dallas, Texas. Forwarding annotation: 'Returned to U.S. / Forward to 1115 Park Ave., Des Mo[ines], Iowa. 7/26/52' with signature. Soldier's service number: 55046284.
metadata.mood_tone: Warm, brotherly, and upbeat; teasing John about still being in Korea, sharing local news and encouragement, with affectionate humor
metadata.legibility_notes: Some ink bleed-through from opposite side of pages partially obscures text, particularly on pages 1 and 2. Most words are legible with close reading. A few words require guessing.
metadata.historical_context: John Hanifen is serving in Korea during the Korean War (1950-1953) as a PFC in the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion. Bob references the GI Bill ('G.I. bill of rights') and encourages John to use it for college at Iowa University. Bob mentions a fellow Dallas man finally being drafted and leaving July 16. Letter was forwarded after being returned to the U.S., indicating John may have rotated back stateside.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS (printed/written in green ink):
After Five Days Return to
R.E. HANIFEN
3614 Noble St.
DALLAS, TEXAS

POSTMARK: DALLAS, TEX. / JUL 16 / 11-AM / 1952 / 8

STAMPS: Two 3-cent United States Postage Thomas Jefferson stamps

VIA AIR MAIL

ADDRESSEE:
P.F.C. John R. Hanifen 55046284
Co. C, 439 Eng. Com. Bn.
APO 301 c/o P.M.
San Francisco, Calif.

FORWARDING ANNOTATION (rubber stamp / handwritten):
Returned to U.S.
Forward to 1115
Park Ave. Des Mo[ines]
Iowa
7/26/52
[signature]

July 15, 1952

Dear John,

     What are you doing in Korea, running the war all by yourself. You have been there long enough. I guess there isn't any one left to draft. But I have good news for you, there is a guy here in Dallas who have been slipping by for a long time and they finally got him, he leaves the 16th of July. I sure they let you go home some time, & figured you should be out for good at least by the 1st of November.

I am still working for the Texas Co. we sell Texaco gas

and Havoline oil. It sure hot here in Dallas hasn't rained for 2 months. I was reading the papers the other day and it says all G.I.s in Korea are eligible for the G.I. bill of rights. You should go to Iowa Uni. for a semester, would do you some good and you could also have a lot of fun. I am still planning on Iowa for Christmas, hope it doesn't snow like it did when you and me came for home in 1948 from Texas. I bought a Des Moines paper, thought this article would interest you a lot more than my writing

sure glad Lew beat Chadwick.
Well John keep your eyes open and maybe the Golden Gate will appear sooner than you think. I hope it does. Drop me a line some time when have the day off.

But so long for now
     Love Your Brother
          Bob

[Photograph enclosed: Black and white photo of a young woman in a dark one-piece swimsuit, seated on what appears to be a wooden diving board or dock, with water and stairs/hillside visible in the background. She is smiling. No writing visible on the photo front.]

LEW JENKINS WINS 75-LAP STOCK CLASSIC BEFORE 3,753

Disregarding the fact that he wasn't one of the leading contenders, Lew Jenkins Friday night roared through 75 laps of blinding dust and spinning cars to win the Pioneer Raceways mid-summer stock car championship before 3,753 fans.

Jenkins, who was all but overlooked in the pre-race forecasts, grabbed the lead from Chris Skadal on the thirty-eighth lap and held it. Fine driving in all of the races made for an unusually small number of collisions.

Skadal, the top driver at Pioneer, led the field through most of the first 38 laps. His stiffest competition came from Wayne Chadwick who held second place until Jenkins made his bid from the No. 3 spot.

From that point on, Jenkins was hounded all the way to the checkered flag by Chadwick and Lefty Robinson, who finished third. At the 50-lap mark Robinson made his big bid, riding Jenkins' rear fender for three laps before falling behind.

Chadwick went back into second place on the sixty-seventh lap and held it to the finish. Skadal had a brush with W. R. Newton on the sixty-third lap and finished sixth.

Main Event—(20 cars, 75 laps) 1. Lew Jenkins. 2. Wayne Chadwick. 3. Lefty Robinson. 4. Jack Thompson. 5. A. DeCarlo. 6. Chris Skadal. Time: 25:51.75.

Semi-Main Event—(20 cars, 30 laps) 1. Keith Lookabaugh. 2. Lee Speck. 3. Al Burherer. 4. John Plum. 5. Bob Huggins. 6. Jim Wilkinson. Time: 11:23.39.

Consolation Race—(13 cars, 20 laps) 1. Bob Bish. 2. Louis Slauson. 3. Clyde Bedford. 4. Newt Bartholmew. 5. Don Burch. 6. Ray Cooper. Time: 7:53.21.

---

BRIGHT WILL PLAY WITH ROAD KINGS

Bobby Grund, Des Moines sports promoter, said Friday Drake grid star Johnny Bright has signed a contract to play pro basketball for him if he plays basketball next season.

Bright played with Grund's Harlem Road Kings briefly last spring.

Bright added, however, "I just received approval of my enrollment in Mount Royal college at Calgary, a branch of the University of Alberta, and I hope to do graduate work this winter."

Bright will play football for a Calgary pro team this fall.

---

LONG SHOTS WIN AT INGLEWOOD

INGLEWOOD, CAL. (AP) — Longshots set off an explosion under the favorites at Hollywood Park Friday, with Admiral Drake making the biggest noise in winning the $50,000 American handicap and sending Intent, the heavy favorite, reeling into third place.

De Anza upset the form [remainder cut off]

full_transcription:
July 15, 1952

Dear John,

     What are you doing in Korea, running the war all by yourself. You have been there long enough. I guess there isn't any one left to draft. But I have good news for you, there is a guy here in Dallas who have been slipping by for a long time and they finally got him, he leaves the 16th of July. I sure they let you go home some time, & figured you should be out for good at least by the 1st of November.

I am still working for the Texas Co. we sell Texaco gas and Havoline oil. It sure hot here in Dallas hasn't rained for 2 months. I was reading the papers the other day and it says all G.I.s in Korea are eligible for the G.I. bill of rights. You should go to Iowa Uni. for a semester, would do you some good and you could also have a lot of fun. I am still planning on Iowa for Christmas, hope it doesn't snow like it did when you and me came for home in 1948 from Texas. I bought a Des Moines paper, thought this article would interest you a lot more than my writing

sure glad Lew beat Chadwick.
Well John keep your eyes open and maybe the Golden Gate will appear sooner than you think. I hope it does. Drop me a line some time when have the day off.

But so long for now
     Love Your Brother
          Bob

[Enclosures: photograph of young woman in swimsuit on dock; newspaper clipping from Des Moines paper about Lew Jenkins winning 75-lap stock car race at Pioneer Raceways, plus secondary articles about Johnny Bright and Inglewood horse racing]
summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his brother John, a PFC serving in Korea with the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion, from Dallas on July 15, 1952. Bob teases John about still being in Korea, shares that he is still working for the Texas Company (Texaco) selling gas and Havoline oil, and notes the severe Dallas drought. He encourages John to use the GI Bill to attend Iowa University when he returns, and mentions his own plans to visit Iowa for Christmas. Bob encloses a Des Moines newspaper clipping about a stock car race won by Lew Jenkins (over Wayne Chadwick) at Pioneer Raceways, as well as what appears to be a photo of a young woman — the letter was later forwarded to 1115 Park Ave., Des Moines, Iowa, suggesting John had rotated back to the United States by late July 1952.
